issue: Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process

entries.position_summary: The United States acknowledges the NPT’s strong record but notes the review process has been uneven, sometimes fostering division. It supports improving and strengthening the review process to better serve and unite the regime.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Cluster Three Specific Issue: Strengthened Review Process Statement as delivered by Mark Goodman, Senior Scientist, Office of Multilateral Nuclear and Security Affairs, Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation, U.S. Department of State Geneva, Switzerland July 30, 2024 Thank you Chair, The Non-Proliferation Treaty has served us well for more than five decades, making inestimable contributions to international security and development. Its record is undeniable. Sadly, however, the NPT review process has more of a mixed record, sometimes bringing us together to strengthen the NPT regime, sometimes highlighting divisions and stoking our frustration. I think we all agree that it could work better.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports strengthening the NPT review process, submitting proposals outlining improvements and endorsing the Chair’s draft recommendations and compromise measures. It believes these have broad support and provide a solid basis for consensus.
entries.quote: 1. The United States submitted a working paper1 to the meeting of the working group on further strengthening the review process of the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, held on 24 to 28 July 2023, which identified several areas in which the review process could be improved and laying out options and issues for consideration by States parties to the Non-Proliferation Treaty. The present working paper provides an update based on the draft recommendations of the Chair (NPT/CONF.2026/WG.I/CRP.2/Rev.1), which the Chair later submitted as a working paper to the first session (held in 2023) of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT/CONF.2026/PC.I/WP.34) and on the deliberations of the working group, including the compromise proposal submitted by four States at the end of the working group’s deliberations. Collectively, these proposals had broad support and provide a solid basis for reaching consensus on measures to strengthen the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports reforms to strengthen and streamline the NPT review process—reducing duplication across meetings, enforcing time limits in plenaries, and enabling interactive discussions of national reports—but regrets that some States Parties blocked consensus on the chair’s draft decision. It is ready to support these measures, provided other nuclear-weapon states do likewise.
entries.quote: Finally, Chair, we regret the fact that a few States Parties were unwilling to work towards consensus on your draft decision on strengthening the review process, which the United States was ready to support. In a time of constrained resources, the United States has made clear our position on the need to reform multilateral institutions to be more effective and efficient. We continue to support good ideas, such as proposals for reducing duplication across meetings, for adhering to strict time limits during plenary sessions, and for interactive discussion of national reports, so long as all other nuclear-weapon States do the same. We deeply regret, despite the best efforts of many of us to get this done, not all were willing to do so.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a working group to strengthen the NPT review process, noting broad member support despite differing substantive priorities. It welcomes the chair’s elements paper circulated during the PrepCom.
entries.quote: to hold a working group aimed at improving the review process. Over the course of this review cycle, Member States have clearly articulated the need for strengthening the review process, regardless of differences in our substantive priorities. We appreciate that the chair has drafted and circulated an elements paper on this important topic during this PrepCom.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates improving the effectiveness of the NPT review process by enhancing the Preparatory Committee’s role in setting the agenda for the Review Conference. This indicates support for a more structured and outcome-oriented review cycle.
entries.quote: • improving our use of the Preparatory Committee to set the agenda for the Review Conference; and

entries.position_summary: The country advocates strengthening the NPT review process to build trust and credibility and to ensure coordinated, continuous efforts across all three pillars. It calls for seizing the current opportunity to shape the future of the review process.
entries.quote: Chair, Much work has already gone into efforts to strengthen the review process. We all have a special opportunity to shape the future of this review process that builds trust and credibility and ensures coordinated and continuous efforts across all three pillars. Let us seize this moment.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports reforms to make the NPT review process more effective and efficient, including reducing duplication across meetings, enforcing strict plenary time limits, and enabling interactive discussion of national reports. It is willing to back good ideas if all Nuclear-Weapon States also commit to these improvements.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States has made clear the need to reform multilateral institutions to be more effective and efficient. We welcome the opportunity to have a robust discussion on strengthening the review process, including proposals for reducing duplication across meetings, for adhering to strict time limits during plenary sessions, and for interactive discussion of national reports. The United States can support good ideas to make this process work better – a goal I believe we all share – if all Nuclear Weapons States do the same.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports holding discussions of national reports in the current review cycle, clarifying that this would not require multiple reports or repeated discussions per cycle. It also encourages the PrepCom to recommend that the RevCon adopt additional Working Group proposals to strengthen the review process, following the 2000 RevCon precedent.
entries.quote: In that light, we could clarify that, at least in our initial efforts, the proposal for discussion of national reports would not require submitting multiple reports in each cycle or multiple discussions of the same national report, a concern voiced by one delegation during last year’s Working Group. For our part, the United States would hope to hold such discussions in this current review cycle. This PrepCom or, more realistically, the next could also recommend that the RevCon adopt other Working Group proposals on the strengthened review process, as the 2000 RevCon did.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports a working group to enhance and structure the NPT review process, identify best practices, and promote constructive engagement to narrow substantive differences.
entries.quote: 2. The United States welcomed the establishment of the working group as an opportunity to consider practical ways to improve the existing review process and to identify and reinforce best practices from recent experience. This working group can still help to structure the review process in ways that foster constructive engagement to reduce substantive differences.

entries.position_summary: The statement argues that duplication across the NPT Preparatory Committee cluster groups, Main Committees, and subsidiary bodies undermines the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process, producing repetitive talking points and limiting interactive deliberations. It supports reforms to enhance effectiveness, efficiency, transparency, accountability, coordination, and continuity, and to better elucidate cross-cutting linkages among the Treaty’s pillars.
entries.quote: 4. The mandate for the working group was “to discuss and make recommendations to the Preparatory Committee on measures that would improve the effectiveness, efficiency, transparency, accountability, coordination and continuity of the review process of the Treaty”. The duplication in coverage of substantive issues across the three cluster groups of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the three Main Committees of the Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, and more so between the Main Committees and their subsidiary bodies, impedes the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process. This leads to repetition of well-known talking points and takes time away from interactive deliberations among States parties. While there are linkages among the three pillars that these clusters and the Main Committees represent, and those pillars are in many ways mutually reinforcing, the current structure has not proven effective at elucidating those connections or at addressing other cross-cutting issues.
